one_liner: Early-warning system for supply chain planners
domain_context:
# SME notes: Mid-size manufacturing supply chain operations

- **SME**: Former VP of Supply Chain, 18 years in industrial manufacturing
- **Date captured**: 2026-07-08

## Domain vocabulary
- **S&OP (Sales & Operations Planning)**: the weekly/monthly cross-functional
  meeting where demand forecasts, supply plans, and inventory risk are
  reconciled - the meeting PulseBoard's summary view is built for.
- **Stockout**: running out of a SKU needed to fulfill a confirmed order.
- **Expedite shipment**: an emergency, above-standard-cost shipment used to
  cover a stockout after the fact - the cost this product aims to reduce.
- **SKU**: stock-keeping unit - a specific trackable inventory item.

## Regulatory / compliance constraints
None specific to this domain beyond standard data-handling practices for
customer order data - no PII/PHI-equivalent regulatory regime applies here.

## What "good" looks like by industry standard
Best-in-class planners in this segment target under 2 hours/week on manual
reconciliation and near-zero surprise stockouts (i.e., every stockout was
flagged with enough lead time to expedite calmly rather than scramble).

## Common failure modes in this domain
- Alert fatigue from noisy, low-precision automated systems - once trust is
  lost, planners revert to manual checking even if the tool is objectively
  faster
- Systems that surface "risk" without a clear recommended action get
  ignored during time-pressured S&OP prep

vision.problem_statement: Supply chain planners at mid-size manufacturers spend 8-10 hours a week manually reconciling demand forecasts across disconnected spreadsheets and ERP exports, discovering stockout risk only after it has already affected a customer order. There is no single place that surfaces "what's about to go wrong" early enough to act on it.
target_users: Demand planners at manufacturers with 3-10 distribution centers
Supply chain ops managers who own the weekly S&OP review
vision.vision_statement: PulseBoard becomes the early-warning system every planner checks before their first coffee - surfacing the three supply risks that matter most today, in plain language, before they become customer-facing problems.
vision.north_star_metric: Reduction in stockout-driven expedite shipments per quarter, measured per customer account.
non_goals: We are not building a full ERP replacement
We are not optimizing inbound freight routing in v1

research_questions: How do planners currently learn about stockout risk today?
What signals do they trust enough to act on immediately?
Where does the 8-10 hours a week of reconciliation actually go?
insights: Planners check 3-5 separate spreadsheets before every S&OP meeting [source: inputs/interviews/priya_planner_2026-07-10.md]
Trust in automated alerts is low because past tools cried wolf on noisy SKUs [source: inputs/customer_feedback/q2_support_tickets_reconciliation.md]
The real bottleneck is reconciling unit-of-measure mismatches between ERP and demand-planning exports [source: inputs/interviews/priya_planner_2026-07-10.md]

Mid-size manufacturers average 6-9 hours/week per planner on manual reconciliation industry-wide, and trust in automated alerts is the top adoption blocker [source: inputs/secondary_research/gartner_supply_chain_visibility_2026.md]

personas.name: Priya, Senior Demand Planner
personas.description: Owns forecast accuracy for 40 SKUs across 4 distribution centers, currently exports to Excel every Monday

personas.name: Marcus, Ops Manager
personas.description: Runs the weekly S&OP review, needs a one-page risk summary he can trust without re-checking the math

opportunity_areas: Automated reconciliation across ERP and demand-planning exports
Trustworthy, low-noise risk surfacing tied to real customer impact
A single pre-meeting summary view for the weekly S&OP review

options.name: Option A
options.description: Automated Reconciliation First** - Build the ERP/demand-plan reconciliation engine first, treat alerting as a phase-2 layer on top of clean data.

options.name: Option B
options.description: Noise-Reduced Alerting First** - Focus on rebuilding planner trust with a low-noise, high-precision alert system, treating reconciliation as a background improvement rather than the headline feature.

options.name: Option C
options.description: Full Planning Suite** - Replace the planners' spreadsheet workflow entirely with an integrated planning and alerting suite, competing directly with larger ERP-adjacent platforms.

strategic_bets: We will build noise-reduced alerting first (Option B, the top-scoring option) because Priya's interview and the Q2 support tickets both point to alert trust - not raw detection capability - as the reason planners revert to manual checking [source: inputs/interviews/priya_planner_2026-07-10.md].
If we ship alerts planners actually trust, we believe manual reconciliation time will drop because planners will stop double-checking the system's numbers by hand.
We will still invest in reconciliation accuracy as a supporting workstream, since noisy alerts are often a symptom of the same underlying data mismatches identified in discovery.
success_metrics: Weekly active planners who act on an alert without first manually verifying it in a spreadsheet
Reduction in stockout-driven expedite shipments per quarter (ties to vision's north star metric)
Alert precision rate (true positives / total alerts fired)
strategy.competitive_positioning: PulseBoard positions against the current workaround - manual spreadsheet reconciliation - rather than existing enterprise ERP suites; per the secondary research, the segment's adoption blocker is trust in automation, not lack of an integrated suite, so competing on "more complete platform" (Option C) would answer a question this segment isn't actually asking [source: inputs/secondary_research/gartner_supply_chain_visibility_2026.md].
constraints: Team can ship one major workstream at a time this year - reconciliation and alerting cannot both be first-class investments in v1

No dedicated data science headcount yet for advanced anomaly detection - alert logic must be explainable with simple, auditable rules per the SME's note that planners ignore alerts they can't verify by eye
